analogy: Think of "Challenges to and Restoration of Congress System" through the chapter's own opening images: In this chapter… In Chapter Two we read about the emergence of the Congress system. This system was first challenged during the 1960s. As political competition became more intense, the Congress found it difficult to retain its dominance. It faced challenges from the opposition that... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 74 Politics in India since Independence, 76 Politics in India since Independence, 78 Politics in India since Independence, 80 Politics in India since Independence. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Challenges to and Restoration of Congress System is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that in this chapter… In Chapter Two we read about the emergence of the Congress system.

The text explains that this system was first challenged during the 1960s.

The text explains that as political competition became more intense, the Congress found it difficult to retain its dominance.

The text explains that it faced challenges from the opposition that was more powerful and less divided than before.

The text explains that the Congress also faced challenges from within, as the party could no longer accommodate all kinds of differences.

The text explains that in this chapter we pick the story from where we left it in Chapter Two, in order to • understand how the political transition took place after Nehru; • describe how the opposition unity and the Congress split posed a challenge to Congress dominance; • explain how a new Congress led by Indira Gandhi overcame these challenges; and • analyse how new policies and ideologies facilitated the restoration of the Congress system.

The text explains that originally the election symbol of the Congress was a pair of bullocks.

The text explains that this famous cartoon depicts the changes within the Congress leading to a head- on confrontation in the 22nd year after Independence.

The text explains that challenge of Political Succession Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru passed away in May 1964.

The text explains that this had generated a lot of speculation about the usual question of succession: after Nehru, who?

The text explains that but in a newly independent country like India, this situation gave rise to a more serious question: after Nehru, what?

The text explains that the second question arose from the serious doubts that many outsiders had about whether India’s democratic experiment will survive after Nehru.

The text explains that it was feared that like so many other newly independent countries, India too would not be able to manage a democratic succession.

The text explains that a failure to do so, it was feared, could lead to a political role for the army.

The text explains that besides, there were doubts if the new leadership would be able to handle the multiple crises that awaited a solution.

The text explains that the 1960s were labelled as the ‘dangerous decade’ when When France or Canada have similar problems, no one talks about failure or disintegration.

The text explains that 5chapter challenges to and restoration of the congress system

The text explains that as discussed in the previous chapter, the country also faced a war with Pakistan in 1965.

The text explains that challenges to and Restoration of the Congress System 75 It took some time before the new Prime Minister could settle down.

The text explains that 76 Politics in India since Independence Fourth General Elections, 1967 The year 1967 is considered a landmark year in India’s political and electoral history.

The text explains that in Chapter Two you read about how the Congress party was the dominant political force throughout the country from 1952 onwards.

The text explains that you will recall from the discussion in Chapter Three and in the previous section of this chapter that the period was fraught with grave economic crisis resulting from successive failure of monsoons, widespread drought, It must have been difficult for her – one woman in a world dominated by men.

The text explains that challenges to and Restoration of the Congress System 77 decline in agricultural production, serious food shortage, depletion of foreign exchange reserves, drop in industrial production and exports, combined with a sharp rise in military expenditure and diversion of resources from planning and economic development.

The text explains that one of the first decisions of the Indira Gandhi government was to devaluate the Indian rupee, under what was seen to be pressure from the US.

The text explains that the government saw the protests as a law and order problem and not as expressions of people’s problems.

The text explains that you will read in the next chapter about how a group of communists who separated from the Communist Party of India (Marxist) to form the Communist Party of India (Marxist-Leninist) led armed agrarian struggles and organised peasant agitations.

The text explains that bhim Singh knew that if the party candidate won from this constituency, that candidate would become a minister and thus, he would have direct contacts with a minister for the first time!

The text explains that he kept telling people that the jagirdar would help build the village school and use his resources for the development of the locality.

The text explains that 78 Politics in India since Independence government.

The text explains that many contemporary political observers described the election results as a ‘political earthquake’.The Congress did manage to get a majority in the Lok Sabha, but with its lowest tally of seats and share of votes since 1952.

The text explains that sahay in Bihar. ...in India, as present trends continue… maintenance of an ordered structure of society is going to slip out of reach of an ordered structure of civil government and the army will be only alternative source of authority and order. …the great experiment of developing India within a democratic framework has failed.

The text explains that challenges to and Restoration of the Congress System 79 Is non-Congressism relevant today?

The text explains that in two other States defections prevented it from forming a government.

The text explains that in the other eight States, coalition governments consisting of different non-Congress parties were formed.

The text explains that since no single party had got majority, various non- Congress parties came together to form joint legislative parties (called Samyukt Vidhayak Dal in Hindi) that supported non-Congress governments.

The text explains that that is why these governments came to be described as SVD governments.

The text explains that the SVD government in Bihar, for instance, included the two socialist parties – SSP and the PSP – along with the CPI on the left and Jana Sangh on the right.

The text explains that a cartoonist’s reading of Charan Singh’s attempt to build a United Front of non-communist parties in 1974 Credit: Kutty What’s so unusual in hung assemblies and coalition governments?

The text explains that challenges to and Restoration of the Congress System 81 Defection Another important feature of the politics after the 1967 election was the role played by defections in the making and unmaking of governments in the States.

The text explains that after the 1967 general election, the breakaway Congress legislators played an important role in installing non-Congress governments in three States - Haryana, Madhya Pradesh and Uttar Pradesh.

The text explains that later, the Constitution was amended to prevent defections.

The text explains that most non- Congress coalition governments in the States did not survive for long.

The text explains that gradually, however, Indira Gandhi attempted to assert her position within the government and the party.

The text explains that she launched a series of initiatives to give the government policy a Left orientation.

The text explains that challenges to and Restoration of the Congress System 83 public distribution of food grains, land reforms and provision of house sites to the rural poor.

The text explains that 84 Politics in India since Independence History … is replete with instances of the tragedy that overtakes democracy when a leader who has risen to power on the crest of a popular wave or with the support of a democratic organisation becomes a victim of political narcissism and is egged on by a coterie of unscrupulous sycophants…...

The text explains that what were the factors which led to the popularity of Indira Gandhi’s Government in the early 1970s?

The text explains that what does the term ‘syndicate’ mean in the context of the Congress party of the sixties?

The text explains that what role did the Syndicate play in the Congress party?

The text explains that discuss the major issue which led to the formal split of the Congress Party in 1969.

The text explains that read the passage and answer the questions below: …Indira Gandhi changed the Congress into highly cerntalised and undemocratic party organisation, from the earlier federal, democratic and ideological formation that Nehru had led…..But this… could not have happened had not Indira Gandhi changed the entire nature of politics.

The text explains that this new, populist politics turned political ideology ……. into a mere electoral discourse, use of various slogans not meant to be translated into government policies…… During its great electoral victories in early 1970s, amidst the celebration, the Congress party as a political organisation died….. — Sudipta Kaviraj (a) What according to the author is the difference between the strategies of Nehru and Indira Gandhi?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Politics in India since Independence, Chapter 5: Challenges to and Restoration of Congress System

Do not add outside examples to Challenges to and Restoration of Congress System; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
